analogy: Think of "Transport and Communication" through the chapter's own opening images: Fundamentals of Human Geography 70 Unit-III Chapter-8 International Trade You are already familiar with the term “trade” as a tertiary activity which you have studied in Chapter 7 of this book. You know that trade means the voluntary exchange of goods and services. In certain... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: GATEWAYS OF INTERNATIONAL TRADE, Transport and Communication, Transport, Communication. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Transport and Communication is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that fundamentals of Human Geography 70 Unit-III Chapter-8 International Trade You are already familiar with the term “trade” as a tertiary activity which you have studied in Chapter 7 of this book.

The text explains that you know that trade means the voluntary exchange of goods and services.

The text explains that in certain places, people barter their goods.

The text explains that for both the parties trade is mutually beneficial.

The text explains that trade may be conducted at two levels: international and national.

The text explains that international trade is the exchange of goods and services among countries across national boundaries.

The text explains that countries need to trade to obtain commodities, they cannot produce themselves or they can purchase elsewhere at a lower price.

The text explains that the initial form of trade in primitive societies was the barter system, where direct exchange of goods took place.

The text explains that in this system if you were a potter and were in need of a plumber, you would have to look for a plumber who would be in need of pots and you could exchange your pots for his plumbing service.

The text explains that 8.1: Two women practising barter system in Jon Beel Mela Every January after the harvest season Jon Beel Mela takes place in Jagiroad, 35 km away from Guwahati and it is possibly the only fair In India, where barter system is still alive.

The text explains that a big market is organised during this fair and people from various tribes and communi- ties exchange their products.

The text explains that the difficulties of barter system were overcome by the introduction of money.

The text explains that in the olden times, before paper and coin currency

The text explains that international Trade 71 came into being, rare objects with very high intrinsic value served as money, like, flintstones, obsidian, cowrie shells, tiger’s paws, whale’s teeth, dogs teeth, skins, furs, cattle, rice, peppercorns, salt, small tools, copper, silver and gold.

The text explains that the word salary comes from the Latin word Salarium which means payment by salt.

The text explains that as in those times producing salt from sea water was unknown and could only be made from rock salt which was rare and expensive.

The text explains that people then spent most of their resources on basic necessities – food and clothes.

The text explains that with well- developed transportation and communication systems, no country is willing to forego the benefits derived from participation in international trade.

The text explains that basis of International Trade (i) Difference in national resources: The world’s national resources are unevenly distributed because of differences in their physical make up i.e. geology, relief soil and climate.

The text explains that (a) Geological structure: It determines the mineral resource base and topographical differences ensure diversity of crops and animals raised.

The text explains that (b) Mineral resources: They are unevenly distributed the world over.

The text explains that the availability of mineral resources provides the basis for industrial development.

The text explains that by developing such capital intensive industries in developing countries, the industrial nations ensure import of food stuffs, minerals and create markets for their finished products.

The text explains that balance of trade and balance of payments have serious implications for a country’s economy.

The text explains that with the development of transport and communication systems goods and services can travel faster and farther than ever before.

The text explains that dumping Dumping Dumping Dumping Dumping The practice of selling a commodity in two countries at a price that differs for reasons not related to costs is called dumping.

The text explains that it sets the rules for the global trading system and resolves disputes between its member nations.

The text explains that wTO also covers trade in services, such as telecommunication and banking, and others issues such as intellectual rights.

The text explains that it is also argued that issues of health, worker’s rights, child labour and environment are ignored.

The text explains that as countries compete to trade more, production and the use of natural resources spiral up, resources get used up faster than they can be replenished.

The text explains that think of some reasons why dumping is becoming a serious concern among trading nations?

The text explains that types of port on the basis of specialised functions: (i) Oil Ports: These ports deal in the processing and shipping of oil.

The text explains that choose the right answer from the four alternatives given below.

The text explains that (v) Naval Ports: These are ports which have only strategic importance.

The text explains that these ports serve warships and have repair workshops for them.

The text explains that kochi and Karwar are examples of such ports in India.

The text explains that (i) Most of the world’s great ports are classified as: (a) Naval Ports (c) Comprehensive Ports (b) Oil Ports (d) Industrial Ports (ii) Which one of the following continents has the maximum flow of global trade?

The text explains that (a) Asia (c) Europe (b) North America (d) Africa 2.

The text explains that (ii) Why is it detrimental for a nation to have negative balance of payments?

The text explains that (iii) What benefits do nations get by forming trading blocs?

The text explains that give a classification of ports on the basis of their location.

The text explains that (ii) How do nations gain from International Trade?

Source: NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography, Chapter 8: Transport and Communication
